1: ʿaṣmênû ûḇĕśārênû ʾănaḥnû — ‘We are your bone and your flesh.’ The kinship-solidarity formula (ʿeṣem ûḇāśār) is the ancient Near Eastern bond of shared physical identity between persons who belong to the same clan or household. Laban uses it of Jacob (Gen 29:14); Abimelech’s men use it of their kin (Judg 9:2); David will use it of all Israel when he appeals for their return after Absalom’s defeat (2 Sam 19:12-13). The formula is the verbal enactment of covenant kinship: ‘we are one body.’ All the tribes present themselves to David as his kin, dissolving the Ishbosheth-era tribal divisions.
2: ‘You were the one who led Israel out to battle and brought them home’ — the language of military shepherding, using yāṣāʾ wāḇôʾ (going out and coming in) as the idiom for military leadership (Num 27:17; Deut 31:2; 1 Sam 18:16). The defining qualification they cite is practical: he was the effective military leader even when another was titular king. YHWH’s appointment: ‘You will shepherd (tirʿeh, H7462) my people Israel and will be ruler (nāgîḏ) over Israel.’ The shepherding metaphor (rāʿāh) for kingship and the nāgîḏ designation (YHWH’s designated one, not merely a self-proclaimed king) both frame David’s rule in covenantal rather than political terms.
3: wayyikrot lāhem hammelek dāwid bĕrît bĕḥeḇrôn lip̄nê YHWH — ‘King David made a covenant with them before YHWH.’ The pan-Israelite anointing at Hebron is David’s third anointing: first by Samuel at Bethlehem (1 Sam 16:13), then by Judah at Hebron (2 Sam 2:4), now by all Israel at Hebron. Three anointings completing the māšaḥ (anointing) sequence — the threefold consecration establishing beyond all ambiguity that this is YHWH’s chosen king. The covenant (bĕrîṯ) made before YHWH frames the political union as a covenant relationship with YHWH as witness and guarantor.
4: David was thirty years old when he became king — the same age at which Levites began their sanctuary service (Num 4:3,23,30,35,39,43,47). Josephus interprets this as a providential alignment. Jesus begins his ministry at ‘about thirty years of age’ (Luke 3:23). Thirty as the age of public inauguration for covenantal service — the Levitical completion of formation, the Davidic beginning of reign, the messianic commencement of ministry — is a consistent pattern. David’s total reign is forty years: seven and a half in Hebron over Judah, thirty-three in Jerusalem over all Israel.
5: The precision of seven years and six months in Hebron vs. thirty-three years in Jerusalem encodes the theology of the unified monarchy: the majority of the reign is from Zion, the city that YHWH will choose. The numbers 7.5 + 33 = 40.5, typically rounded to 40 — the covenant number of completeness (forty years in the wilderness; forty days of Moses on Sinai; forty days of Jesus in the wilderness). David’s reign is shaped by the covenant arithmetic of complete faithfulness within YHWH’s appointed duration.
6: The Jebusites taunt: ‘You will not get in here; even the blind and the lame can ward you off.’ (lōʾ-tāḇôʾ hēnnāh kî ʾim-hĕsîrĕḵā hāʿiwwĕrîm wĕhappissĕḥîm). The phrase has generated multiple interpretations for the cryptic proverb ‘the blind and the lame will keep David out.’ The Jebusites’ mockery claims the city’s natural defenses are so strong that even the physically disabled defenders could hold it. The taunt is answered by David’s capture — YHWH’s ironic reversal: the city that cannot be taken is taken; the despised king takes the ‘untakeable’ stronghold.
7: wayyilkoḏ dāwid ʾet-mĕṣuḏaṯ ṣiyyôn hîʾ ʿîr dāwid — ‘David captured the stronghold of Zion; this is the city of David.’ The first occurrence of ṣiyyôn (H6726, Zion) in the historical narrative establishes the theological geography that will dominate the OT and NT: the mount of YHWH’s presence, the place of the ark, the city of the anointed king. The identification of Zion with ‘the city of David’ (ʿîr dāwid) links the cosmic sanctuary-mountain with the Davidic covenant — this place belongs to the anointed king and his line.
8: ‘Whoever attacks the Jebusites must go up through the water tunnel (ṣinnôr)’ — the water-shaft that Warren’s archaeological exploration in 1867 identified as the access to Jerusalem’s water supply. The technical term ṣinnôr (H6794, conduit/water-shaft) is unique here; its meaning has been debated. The narrator adds: ‘Therefore they say, the blind and the lame shall not enter the house’ — an etiological note connecting the anti-Jebusite taunt to a post-conquest proverb. The water-shaft entrance is the hidden access through which the impossible capture became possible — a detail that resonates with the OT pattern of YHWH using unexpected means to accomplish his purposes.
9: David occupies the stronghold and calls it ʿîr dāwid (the city of David), then builds up the city from the millōʾ (H4407, filling/terrace, the retaining wall system) inward. The millōʾ appears repeatedly as a major construction element in Jerusalem (1 Kgs 9:15,24; 11:27; 2 Kgs 12:20) — apparently a large platform or terrace supporting the upper city structures. Jerusalem’s topography required massive earthwork to create buildable surfaces; David begins the infrastructure that Solomon will complete.
10: wayyēleḵ dāwid hālôḵ wĕgāḏôl waYHWH ʾĕlōhê ṣĕḇāʾôṯ ʿimmô — ‘David grew steadily greater, for YHWH God of hosts was with him.’ The divine presence formula — ‘YHWH was with him’ (ʿimmô) — is the consistent theological explanation for David’s prosperity (1 Sam 16:18; 18:14; 2 Sam 7:3,9). YHWH ʾĕlōhê ṣĕḇāʾôṯ (YHWH God of Hosts) — the full title emphasizing the divine warrior who commands the heavenly armies — first sustained use here in 2 Samuel, reflecting the military context of Jerusalem’s capture and the Philistine defeats to follow.
11: Hiram king of Tyre sends cedar timber (ʾarzê halĕḇānôn), carpenters, and stonemasons, and builds David’s palace. The Phoenician craftsmen and materials that will be central to Solomon’s temple-building (1 Kgs 5-7) begin here with the royal palace. Cedar (ʾerez, H730) — the prestige building material of the ancient Near East, its fragrance and durability associated with royalty throughout the region — signals David’s elevation to peer-king status recognized by the major Levantine trading power.
12: wayyēḏaʿ dāwid kî-hĕkînô YHWH lĕmelek ʿal-yiśrāʾēl wĕkî niśśēʾ mamlaktô baʿăḇûr ʿammô yiśrāʾēl — ‘David recognized that YHWH had established (hĕkîn, H3559 — from kûn, to establish/confirm/make firm) him as king over Israel and had elevated (niśśēʾ) his kingdom for the sake of his people Israel.’ The verb kûn (to establish/confirm) is the key term of the Davidic covenant that will appear six times in ch7. David’s theological recognition — connecting his establishment to YHWH’s purpose for Israel rather than personal achievement — is the covenant-consciousness that characterizes his kingship.
13: David takes more concubines and wives in Jerusalem; more sons and daughters are born. The royal harem expansion in Jerusalem is both a political act (alliances) and a covenant warning — the law of the king in Deut 17:17 prohibits the king from multiplying wives (lōʾ yarbeh-lô nāšîm). The narrator records without comment what will become a source of crisis: Solomon’s foreign wives who turn his heart (1 Kgs 11:1-8) and Absalom’s future violation of David’s concubines (2 Sam 16:21-22) are seeds planted here. The king who multiplies what the Torah prohibits is sowing for a later harvest.
14: The list of David’s sons born in Jerusalem begins with Shammua, Shobab, Nathan, Solomon (šĕmôʿāh, šôḇāḇ, nāṯān, šĕlōmōh). Nāṯān (Nathan, ‘he has given’) — not the prophet but a son — will appear in Luke 3:31 as an ancestor of Mary in the Lukan genealogy, providing the non-Solomonic Davidic line through which Christ’s legal Davidic descent passes on the maternal side. Šĕlōmōh (Solomon, from šālôm, peace) — his name encoding the peace-era covenant blessing — will be the immediate fulfillment of the Davidic covenant promise.
15: The list of David’s Jerusalem sons continues: Ibhar, Elishua, Nepheg, and Japhia. ʾelîšûaʿ (Elishua, ‘my God is salvation’) — another throne-name encoding theology; ʾelîšāmaʿ and ʾelîyāḏāʿ (vv16-17) similarly encode the divine name with covenantal attributes. Royal name-theology in the ancient Near East embedded the king’s relationship to the deity in the names of his sons — these are not random names but theological declarations about YHWH’s relationship to the Davidic house.
16: Elishama, Eliada, and Eliphelet complete the Jerusalem-born sons list. ʾelîyāḏāʿ (Eliada, ‘God has known’, H450) uses the covenant-knowledge verb yāḏaʿ — the same verb in YHWH’s covenant formula ‘you only have I known (yāḏaʿtî) of all the families of the earth’ (Amos 3:2). The royal sons’ names encode the covenant relationship between YHWH and the Davidic house: God knows, God is salvation, God has given. These names are a liturgical confession distributed across the royal household.
17: When the Philistines heard that David had been anointed king over Israel, ‘all the Philistines went up to search for David’ — the anointing triggers the military threat. The same pattern as Jesus: at the announcement of his birth, Herod sends soldiers (Matt 2:16); at his baptism-anointing, the Spirit drives him into the wilderness for the Satan-confrontation (Mark 1:12). The anointing of YHWH’s king is the signal for the powers opposed to the covenant to mobilize. David’s inquiring of YHWH (v19) before engaging is the immediate covenant response.
18: The Philistines deploy in the Valley of Rephaim (ʿemeq rĕpāʾîm, H7497 — valley of the Rephaim). The Rephaim were the pre-Israelite giant warriors of Canaan (Deut 2:11,20; 3:11,13) — the valley associated with their remnants is the site of both Philistine deployments (vv18 and 22). The choice of this valley may signal the Philistines’ alignment with the ancient enemies of Israel’s inheritance, or may simply be the tactically favorable position. David’s two victories here demonstrate that YHWH has indeed ‘burst through’ the enemies of his anointed.
19: wayyišʾal dāwid baYHWH lēʾmōr haʾeʿăleh ʾel-pĕlištîm — ‘David inquired of YHWH: shall I go up against the Philistines?’ The formal consultation of YHWH before military action — using the oracle mechanism (šāʾal, H7592, to ask/inquire) — is the consistent marker of covenant-dependent leadership. Saul’s failure at the same point (1 Sam 28: Saul cannot get an answer from YHWH and turns to the medium) is the negative foil. YHWH’s answer is direct: ʿălēh kî nāṯōn ʾettēn ʾet-happĕlištîm bĕyāḏeḵā — the double infinitive absolute ‘I will certainly give’ indicates emphatic certainty.
20: pāraṣ YHWH ʾet-ʾōyĕḇay lĕp̄ānay kĕp̄ereṣ mayim — ‘YHWH has burst through (pāraṣ, H6555) my enemies before me like a bursting of waters.’ The verb pāraṣ (to break through/burst out) conveys irresistible forward movement — the same root in ‘may you break out (ûp̄āraṣtā)’ of the promise to Jacob (Gen 28:14). The name Baal-Perazim (‘Lord of breaking through’) is the site-naming that memorializes YHWH’s action. David attributes the victory entirely to YHWH’s agency: he interprets his military success as YHWH breaking through, not David conquering.
21: The Philistines abandon their idols (ʿaṣabbêhem, H6091 — idols, from a root meaning carved/shaped). David and his men carry them off (wayyiśśāʾēm). The reversal is pointed: in 1 Sam 5-6 the Philistines had captured the ark of YHWH and found YHWH’s superiority demonstrated by the Dagon-disaster; now David captures their idols. The direction of trophy-taking has reversed: in the time of Eli the ark went to Philistia; now the Philistine idols come to Israel. The anointed king’s victory is measured in the trophies of the defeated powers.
22: The Philistines come up a second time to the Valley of Rephaim. The repetition of the attack after the first defeat tests whether David will again inquire of YHWH or trust his previous tactics. The consistent inquiry-before-action pattern is the model for covenant-dependent leadership — never assuming past success authorizes present action without fresh divine guidance. Saul’s pattern was repetition of previous tactics; David’s pattern is fresh inquiry each time.
23: YHWH’s different tactical instruction for the second battle — ‘Do not go straight up; circle around behind them and come at them from in front of the balsam trees (bĕkāʾîm)’ — demonstrates the non-mechanical nature of YHWH’s guidance. The same enemy, the same valley, a completely different tactic. Bākāʾ trees (H1057, probably balsam or mastic trees) — the wind-movement in their tops will be the supernatural signal. YHWH’s sovereignty over tactics means each engagement requires fresh obedience, not tactical formulaic repetition.
24: bĕšomʿăḵā ʾet-qôl ṣĕʿāḏāh bĕrāʾšê habbĕkāʾîm — ‘When you hear the sound of marching in the tops of the balsam trees, then act decisively, for YHWH has gone out before you.’ The audible supernatural signal — YHWH’s army marching in the treetops — is the divine guarantee of victory announced before battle. The divine warrior going out before the army (yāṣāʾ lip̄nêḵā) is the theophanic-war pattern: YHWH as the true military commander whose presence precedes the human army. The divine sound in the trees is the audible manifestation of the cosmic war that underlies every covenant military engagement.
25: David does as YHWH commanded and strikes the Philistines from Geba to Gezer — the full extent of the Philistine deployment zone in the central Shephelah corridor. ‘As YHWH commanded him’ (kaʾăšer ṣiwwāh YHWH) — the fulfillment-of-command formula that validates the preceding divine instruction. The bracket ‘YHWH commanded’ (v23) and ‘David did as YHWH commanded’ (v25) encloses the battle account: the human action is the execution of the divine word, not independent military initiative.
